title_ar: مسائل الخلاف في الطهارة في الإنصاف
content_en:
Al-Mardawi's treatment of taharah in Al-Insaf illustrates the work's methodology: for each issue, he presents the multiple opinions transmitted within the Hanbali school, identifies the scholars holding each view, and then renders his judgment on the preponderant (rajih) opinion. Even within a single school, significant differences can exist — differences that originated with Imam Ahmad's multiple transmitted opinions (riwayat), or that arose from the independent ijtihad of later Hanbali scholars.

A classic example is the question of whether used water (musta'mal) can be used for ritual purification. Within the Hanbali school, Imam Ahmad transmitted multiple positions: one opinion holds that used water is pure and purifying (like fresh water), another holds it is pure but not purifying, and a third holds it is even najis (impure). Al-Mardawi evaluates these positions and concludes that the rajih is the middle position — the water is pure (tahir) but not purifying (not tahur) — based on the stronger evidence and the more cohesive position of later Hanbali jurists.

On the question of water contaminated by a dead aquatic animal (such as a fish or a frog), the Hanbali school has multiple transmitted opinions based on the hadith 'Its water is pure and its dead are lawful' (referring to the sea) and its extension by analogy. Al-Mardawi carefully analyzes which aquatic animals are covered by this principle, identifying the distinctions drawn by earlier Hanbali scholars and resolving which categorization is most evidentially sound.

The question of whether wiping leather socks (khuff) in wudu prevents subsequent prayers if the condition that was present at the start of the wiping period changes (such as becoming a resident after traveling) generates multiple Hanbali views. Al-Mardawi presents each view with its reasoning: some scholars hold that the wiping period converts to the shorter resident period immediately upon becoming a resident; others hold that the original wiping continues until its original period expires. Al-Mardawi identifies the rajih as the former view.

The Hanbali school's distinctive position that eating camel meat nullifies wudu is treated with its evidential basis (the hadith of Jabir ibn Samurah in Sahih Muslim: 'Perform wudu after eating camel meat') and the internal school dispute about its scope. Does this apply only to the meat, or also to camel fat, liver, and other parts? Al-Mardawi traces the various Hanbali opinions and concludes that all parts of the camel, including its milk according to one view, are covered.

On menstruation (hayd) — the minimum, maximum, and usual periods, and how to handle irregular or uncertain cases — the Hanbali school has numerous transmitted positions from Imam Ahmad and his students, each based on different readings of the relevant hadiths and different methodological principles. Al-Mardawi's systematic presentation of these disputes and his resolution of them with evidential reasoning provides the student of Hanbali fiqh with a complete map of this complex and practically important area of law.
